\section{Experiments}
\label{sec:experiments}

\noindent\textbf{Code-base:} \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/p-c-m-a-E866/README.md}{\texttt{<Repo>}}
\hfill
\textbf{App-demo:} \href{https://huggingface.co/spaces/anonymous-artifact/pcg-demo}{\texttt{<URL>}}

\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Goal.}
We evaluate whether proof-carrying verification improves agentic reliability beyond both
ordinary no-certificate generation and a recent safety-policy guardrail baseline. The experiments
track five linked questions: whether accepted failures decompose into auditable runtime channels
(R1), whether independent certificate redundancy suppresses false acceptance under adversarial
stress (R2), whether mask-and-replay identifies the responsible component (R3), whether
certificate-derived risk improves control decisions (R4), and what overhead this contract imposes
(R5). Figure~\ref{fig:intro-hero-v4} gives the cross-cutting summary; detailed matrix results are
deferred to Appendix~\ref{appendix:exp_details}.


\subsection{Setup}
\label{sec:exp_setup}

\noindent\textbf{Datasets.}
We use eight tasks that stress different failure modes of agentic grounding:
\href{https://hotpotqa.github.io/}{\texttt{HotpotQA}} for explainable multi-hop QA
\citep{yang2018hotpotqa};
\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/framolfese/2WikiMultihopQA}{\texttt{2WikiMultiHopQA}} for
structured and unstructured multi-hop reasoning with explicit reasoning paths
\citep{ho2020constructing};
\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/next-tat/TAT-QA}{\texttt{TAT-QA}} for hybrid financial
table--text numerical QA \citep{zhu2021tatqa};
\href{https://github.com/OpenBMB/ToolBench}{\texttt{ToolBench}} for tool-use instruction following
and API-style agent behavior \citep{qin2023toollm};
\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/fever/fever}{\texttt{FEVER}} for evidence-backed claim
verification \citep{thorne2018fever};
\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/qiaojin/PubMedQA}{\texttt{PubMedQA}} for biomedical
yes/no/maybe reasoning over abstracts \citep{jin2019pubmedqa};
\href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/McGill-NLP/WebLINX}{\texttt{WebLINX}} for conversational
web navigation with browser-action trajectories \citep{lu2024weblinx}; and a synthetic
adversarial split that perturbs evidence integrity, replay faithfulness, checker behavior, and
coverage to stress the four audit channels. The synthetic construction is described in
Appendix~\ref{appendix:exp_details}.

\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{LLM backends.}
The benchmark spans seven instruction-tuned backends:
\href{https://huggingface.co/microsoft/Phi-3.5-mini-instruct}{\texttt{microsoft/Phi-3.5-mini-instruct}}
(\(\sim\)3.8B, Phi-3.5 family);
\href{https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct}{\texttt{Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct}}
(7B, Qwen2.5 family);
\href{https://huggingface.co/mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3}{\texttt{mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3}}
(7B, Mistral instruction family);
\href{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct}{\texttt{meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct}}
(8B, Llama-3.1 family);
\href{https://huggingface.co/google/gemma-2-9b-it}{\texttt{google/gemma-2-9b-it}}
(9B, Gemma-2 instruction family);
\href{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct}{\texttt{meta-llama/Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct}}
(70B, Llama-3.3 family); and
\href{https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3}{\texttt{deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3}}
(671B total / 37B activated MoE). The five smaller/local backends form the \(5\times8\) local
matrix; \texttt{Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct} and \texttt{DeepSeek-V3} form the 16-cell remote split.
We use \texttt{HF\_TOKEN} only for gated or authenticated model access, specifically for gated
Meta Llama checkpoints and any HF Inference / router calls required by the remote split.

\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Baselines.}
\texttt{No certificate} is the ordinary agentic generation pipeline: retrieve, call tools, delegate,
and answer, but accept without a replayable certificate. \texttt{SHIELDAGENT} is a policy-reasoning
guardrail baseline inspired by verifiable safety-policy shielding over agent trajectories
\citep{shieldagent2025}. It validates safety-policy consistency but does not require the full
PCG-MAS contract: committed evidence hashes, replayable support pipelines, independent support
paths, replay interventions, and certificate-derived risk control. \texttt{PCG-MAS} uses the full
unified certificate and accepts only when the evidence and execution checks pass.

\smallskip
\noindent\textbf{Metrics.}
Tables~\ref{tab:main_six_summary}--\ref{tab:cost_overhead_main} use three metric groups. 
\emph{Table~\ref{tab:main_six_summary}} reports the headline six-cell safety and utility summary:
\(\mathrm{Harm}_{\mathrm{NoCert}}\), \(\mathrm{Harm}_{\mathrm{Shield}}\), and
\(\mathrm{Harm}_{\mathrm{PCG}}\) denote the accepted-claim harm rate under no certificate,
\texttt{SHIELDAGENT}, and \texttt{PCG-MAS}, respectively, where harm means an accepted claim is
wrong, unsupported, or unsafe; \(\mathrm{Cert.\ cov.}\) is the fraction of observed bad accepts
covered by auditable certificate channels; \(\mathrm{Resp.@1}\) is top-1 root-cause accuracy under
mask-and-replay responsibility diagnosis over evidence/tool/schema/memory/policy/delegation
components; and \(\mathrm{Utility}\) is answered-claim utility after selective verification,
escalation, or refusal. \emph{Table~\ref{tab:r1_r4_combined}} aggregates the R1--R4 diagnostics:
\(\mathrm{R1\ audit\ cov.}\) is the finite-sample audit-channel coverage; \(\mathrm{R2\ safety\
gain}\) is the multiplicative harm reduction of \texttt{PCG-MAS} relative to \texttt{No certificate};
\(\mathrm{Shield}\!\rightarrow\!\mathrm{PCG\ gap}\) is the remaining multiplicative harm reduction of
\texttt{PCG-MAS} relative to \texttt{SHIELDAGENT}; \(\mathrm{R3\ resp.\ lift}\) is the percentage-point
gain in responsibility@1 over \texttt{SHIELDAGENT}; and \(\mathrm{R4\ control\ gain}\) is the
multiplicative reduction in harm-weighted operating cost relative to \texttt{SHIELDAGENT}.
\emph{Table~\ref{tab:cost_overhead_main}} reports overhead: \(\mathrm{NoCert\ tok.}\),
\(\mathrm{Shield\ tok.}\), and \(\mathrm{PCG\ tok.}\) are token multipliers relative to the
no-certificate pipeline, while \(\mathrm{Shield\ lat.}\) and \(\mathrm{PCG\ lat.}\) are latency
multipliers. The six main cells are selected to cover model scale and task diversity:
\texttt{phi-3.5-mini}/\texttt{FEVER}, \texttt{qwen2.5-7B}/\texttt{HotpotQA},
\texttt{Llama-3.1-8B}/\texttt{PubMedQA}, \texttt{Gemma-2-9b-it}/\texttt{TAT-QA},
\texttt{Llama-3.3-70B}/\texttt{ToolBench}, and \texttt{deepseek-v3}/\texttt{WebLINX}; the remaining
50 cells appear in Appendix~\ref{appendix:exp_details}.

\input{.../main_six_summary}
\input{.../r1_r4_combined}
\input{.../cost_overhead_main}

\subsection{Results and discussion}
\label{sec:exp_results_discussion}

\noindent\textbf{R1: accepted failures become auditable.}
Figure~\ref{fig:r1-audit-v4} replaces theorem-specific labels with an operational view:
observed bad accepts are compared against the four certificate-failure channels
\texttt{Integrity}, \texttt{Replay}, \texttt{Checker}, and \texttt{Coverage}. Across the displayed
cells, \texttt{PCG-MAS} has the lowest bad-accept rate and exposes which part of the runtime caused
the residual failure, while \texttt{SHIELDAGENT} improves over no certificate but lacks the same
evidence-replay decomposition.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figures/v4/r1_audit_decomposition_v4.pdf}
  \caption{\textbf{R1.} Certified audit decomposition across the fixed three R1--R5 cells:
  \texttt{qwen2.5-7B}/\texttt{HotpotQA}, \texttt{Llama-3.1-8B}/\texttt{PubMedQA}, and
  \texttt{deepseek-v3}/\texttt{WebLINX}. PCG-MAS both lowers bad accepts and decomposes residual
  failures into auditable runtime channels.}
  \label{fig:r1-audit-v4}
\end{figure*}

\noindent\textbf{R2: redundancy improves under adversarial stress.}
Figure~\ref{fig:r2-redundancy-v4} visualizes the same dependency-aware redundancy law over an
adversarial-stress grid. Bubble size encodes certified false-accept risk as redundancy \(k\) and
stress \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{adv}}\) vary. This avoids a visually trivial log-line plot while
preserving the underlying theory: increasing independent support paths contracts risk fastest for
\texttt{PCG-MAS}, less strongly for \texttt{SHIELDAGENT}, and weakest for no-certificate operation.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figures/v4/r2_redundancy_surface_v4.pdf}
  \caption{\textbf{R2.} Redundancy under adversarial stress. The plotted surface evaluates the
  same certified redundancy envelope across \(k\) and \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{adv}}\), showing that
  PCG-MAS achieves the sharpest risk collapse under support-path redundancy.}
  \label{fig:r2-redundancy-v4}
\end{figure*}

\noindent\textbf{R3: failures are localizable.}
Figure~\ref{fig:r3-responsibility-v4} shows that replay interventions make failure diagnosis
recoverable from the certificate trace. \texttt{SHIELDAGENT} improves policy-level safety relative
to no certificate, but \texttt{PCG-MAS} provides a stronger diagnostic object because the verifier can
mask evidence, tool, schema, memory, policy, or delegation components and replay the same trace.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figures/v4/r3_responsibility_v4.pdf}
  \caption{\textbf{R3.} Responsibility ranking under replay interventions. PCG-MAS improves top-1
  root-cause recovery because the accepted certificate records the auditable runtime components
  needed for mask-and-replay diagnosis.}
  \label{fig:r3-responsibility-v4}
\end{figure*}

\noindent\textbf{R4: certificate risk improves control.}
Figure~\ref{fig:r4-control-v4} uses utility versus harm-weighted operating cost rather than a
hard-to-read log-harm/cost curve. The desired operating regime is high utility and low harm-weighted
cost. \texttt{PCG-MAS} shifts decisions toward this regime by using certificate-derived risk to
select among \texttt{Answer}, \texttt{Verify}, \texttt{Escalate}, and \texttt{Refuse}.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figures/v4/r4_control_frontier_v4.pdf}
  \caption{\textbf{R4.} Risk-control frontier. PCG-MAS yields a better utility--harm operating
  point because acceptance, verification, escalation, and refusal are driven by certificate risk
  rather than post-hoc confidence alone.}
  \label{fig:r4-control-v4}
\end{figure*}

\noindent\textbf{R5: certification is measurable but bounded.}
Figure~\ref{fig:r5-overhead-v4} reports token overhead and phase decomposition. \texttt{No certificate}
is cheapest, \texttt{SHIELDAGENT} adds moderate policy-reasoning cost, and \texttt{PCG-MAS} is more
expensive because it adds evidence commitments, replay, redundancy, and diagnosis. This cost is not
hidden: Table~\ref{tab:cost_overhead_main} reports token and latency multipliers, while the appendix
breaks down the remaining cells.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.98\textwidth]{figures/v4/r5_overhead_v4.pdf}
  \caption{\textbf{R5.} Certification overhead. PCG-MAS costs more than a policy-only guardrail, but
  the extra cost buys replayable evidence certificates, independent support paths, and diagnostic
  responsibility.}
  \label{fig:r5-overhead-v4}
\end{figure*}

\noindent\textbf{Takeaway.}
Across the five result families, the comparison is consistent: no-certificate agents are cheapest
but least safe; \texttt{SHIELDAGENT} improves trajectory-level policy safety; \texttt{PCG-MAS} wins
on externally checkable groundedness because it verifies evidence, execution, redundancy,
responsibility, and risk control in a single auditable runtime contract. Appendix~\ref{appendix:exp_details}
contains the complementary 50-cell tables, prompt bank/templates, backend manifest, and local/remote
reconciliation details.
